questions.stem: A child with fever is breathing faster than usual and has nasal flaring. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Reassess respiratory status and oxygenation immediately
Tell the parent the child is probably just warm
Wait until the next temperature check
Focus only on giving antipyretics

questions.rationale: Nasal flaring and tachypnea suggest respiratory distress and require prompt assessment. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Fever does not explain away respiratory distress. - C Delay is unsafe. - D Lowering the fever does not replace breathing assessment.

distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in Tell the parent the child is probably just warm is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for **Why the others are wrong:** - B Fever does not explain away respiratory distress. Reassess respiratory status and oxygenation immediately is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: This answer choice treats Wait until the next temperature check as the key issue, although the nurse should be guided by A child with fever is breathing faster than usual and has nasal flaring. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of oxygenation or ventilation and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Focus only on giving antipyretics, while the decisive cue is Nasal flaring and tachypnea suggest respiratory distress and require prompt assessment. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.

questions.takeaway: Nasal flaring is a pediatric respiratory warning sign.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly has facial droop and one-sided arm weakness. What is the nurse's priority?

options.text: Activate the stroke response process immediately
Recheck in an hour to see whether it improves
Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking
Give pain medication first

questions.rationale: Sudden focal deficits can signal stroke, and rapid response matters because time-sensitive treatment may be available. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Waiting can cost treatment time. - C Rest does not address the emergency. - D Pain control is not the first priority here.

distractorRationales.b: This option overweights Recheck in an hour to see whether it improves, rather than acting on A patient suddenly has facial droop and one-sided arm weakness. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Activate the stroke response process immediately; otherwise neurologic deterioration remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.c: Ask the patient to rest and avoid talking would not address the priority, even though the higher-risk finding is - D Pain control is not the first priority here. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The correct path is Activate the stroke response process immediately, which matches the priority cues instead of a secondary concern.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Give pain medication first, although the nurse should be guided by A patient suddenly has facial droop and one-sided arm weakness. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Activate the stroke response process immediately; otherwise neurologic deterioration remains the active threat.

questions.takeaway: Sudden one-sided weakness is a stroke emergency until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A nurse receives report on four patients. Which patient is UNSTABLE and requires immediate reassessment?

options.text: Post-op appendectomy patient, day 1, HR 92, BP 118/74, mild incisional pain 4/10
Patient with pneumonia, T 38.4°C, HR 88, RR 20, SpO₂ 95% on 3L NC, improving on antibiotics
Patient with GI bleed admitted 6 hours ago: HR 118 (was 88 one hour ago), BP 96/58 (was 118/72), increased bright red rectal bleeding
Patient with COPD exacerbation, SpO₂ 91% on 2L NC (baseline 90–92%), pursed-lip breathing

questions.rationale: The GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from volume loss), BP dropped 22/14 mmHg (hypoperfusion), and bleeding is visibly worsening. This trajectory — not just the absolute values — defines instability. A patient is unstable when vital signs are trending in the wrong direction, indicating the body's compensatory mechanisms are being overwhelmed. The post-op patient (A) is stable and expected. The pneumonia patient (B) is improving on treatment. The COPD patient (D) is at their baseline SpO₂.

distractorRationales.a: This option makes Post-op appendectomy patient, day 1, HR 92, BP 118/74, mild incisional pain 4/10 the focus, and misses that The GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bleeding-risk intervention while bleeding or anticoagulation injury progresses.
distractorRationales.b: This option makes Patient with pneumonia, T 38.4°C, HR 88, RR 20, SpO₂ 95% on 3L NC, improving on the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is The GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so It is less defensible because Patient with GI bleed admitted 6 hours ago: HR 118 (was 88 one hour ago), BP targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Patient with COPD exacerbation, SpO₂ 91% on 2L NC (baseline 90–92%), pursed-lip breathing the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by The GI bleed patient shows a deteriorating trend over one hour: HR increased 30 bpm (compensatory tachycardia from. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so It is less defensible because Patient with GI bleed admitted 6 hours ago: HR 118 (was 88 one hour ago), BP targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: Instability = trending in the wrong direction. HR rising + BP falling + worsening bleeding = active hemorrhagic shock. Always compare current vitals to the baseline/trend, not just absolute values.

questions.stem: A patient with asthma is wheezing loudly, using accessory muscles, and can only say a few words at a time. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The patient is having significant respiratory distress
The wheezing proves the airway is opening well
The patient should rest without reassessment
Talking less always means improvement

questions.rationale: Accessory muscle use and limited speech suggest increased work of breathing and a worsening respiratory pattern. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Loud wheezing does not mean the patient is stable. - C Rest does not replace reassessment. - D Short speech can indicate more, not less, distress.

distractorRationales.b: The reasoning trap in The wheezing proves the airway is opening well is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, without accounting for Speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.c: The patient should rest without reassessment is not the best answer, and misses that Speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The patient is having significant respiratory distress better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: This option overweights Talking less always means improvement, while the decisive cue is A patient with asthma is wheezing loudly, using accessory muscles. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The priority is The patient is having significant respiratory distress, not a step that delays stabilization of oxygenation or ventilation.

questions.takeaway: Speech limitation plus accessory muscle use is a respiratory warning sign.

questions.stem: The nurse has four patients. Which should be seen first?

options.text: A patient with shortness of breath and accessory muscle use
A patient requesting help to the bathroom
A patient asking for a TV remote
A patient waiting for routine teaching

questions.rationale: Breathing difficulty with accessory muscle use is an immediate assessment priority. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Bathroom help is important but less urgent. - C The remote is nonurgent. - D Routine teaching can wait.

distractorRationales.b: This answer choice treats A patient requesting help to the bathroom as the key issue, rather than acting on Breathing problems outrank routine requests. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize A patient with shortness of breath and accessory muscle use; otherwise oxygenation or ventilation remains the active threat.

distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in A patient waiting for routine teaching is that it treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, when the bedside priority is driven by Breathing difficulty with accessory muscle use is an immediate assessment priority. A patient with shortness of breath and accessory muscle use is safer because it addresses oxygenation or ventilation before routine care or reassurance.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue, rather than acting on A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation, not a step that delays stabilization of clinical safety risk.

The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation better matches the nursing priority, so this option would slow the needed intervention.
distractorRationales.d: These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well would not address the priority, without accounting for Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for bedside safety action while clinical safety risk progresses.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg. Moves to a lower-acuity action before the client is clinically stable, so Choosing it can delay renal protection and leave renal injury unresolved.
distractorRationales.c: Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn fits a lower-risk interpretation, although the nurse should be guided by A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The nurse should prioritize Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids; otherwise renal injury remains the active threat.
distractorRationales.d: The clinical error in Teach the client about low-sodium foods is that it moves to teaching or reassurance before the immediate risk is stabilized, but the stem cue is The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids is safer because it addresses renal injury before routine care or reassurance.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Obtain a full allergy history before intervening is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, even though the higher-risk finding is The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for airway and breathing intervention while oxygenation or ventilation progresses.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise. It is less defensible because Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.
distractorRationales.d: Document the event and continue monitoring fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so Choosing it can delay airway and breathing intervention and leave oxygenation or ventilation unresolved.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: This option makes Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for neurologic safety assessment while neurologic deterioration progresses.
distractorRationales.c: This choice focuses on Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes, while the decisive cue is A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so Choosing it can delay neurologic safety assessment and leave neurologic deterioration unresolved.
distractorRationales.d: This option makes Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes the focus, even though the higher-risk finding is Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for neurologic safety assessment while neurologic deterioration progresses.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: This choice focuses on Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes, instead of responding to A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so The priority is Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline, not a step that delays stabilization of circulation or perfusion.
distractorRationales.c: The reasoning trap in Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion is that it does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, when the bedside priority is driven by chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for circulation and perfusion support while circulation or perfusion progresses.
distractorRationales.d: Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response fits a lower-risk interpretation, rather than acting on Chills, low back pain. Normalizes a finding that should change the nurse's priority, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of circulation or perfusion and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: The clinical error in Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster is that it anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, and misses that c diff spore precautions infection. That choice could make the nurse miss the window for infection or sepsis escalation while infection progression progresses.
distractorRationales.c: Place the client on droplet precautions only fits a lower-risk interpretation, while the decisive cue is c diff spore precautions infection. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.
distractorRationales.d: Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel is not the best answer, but the stem cue is c diff spore precautions infection. Anchors on the wrong explanation for the client's cues, so It is less defensible because Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves targets the problem most likely to deteriorate first.

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.

questions.stem: Which action by the nurse best helps prevent a central line-associated bloodstream infection in a hospitalized client?

options.text: Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing.
Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort.
Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures.
Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions.

questions.rationale: Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Using the line casually or leaving it uncapped increases contamination risk. Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone.

distractorRationales.b: Change the dressing only when the client reports discomfort fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so The safer answer is Scrub the hub before each access and maintain a sterile dressing because delaying infection or sepsis escalation can worsen infection progression.
distractorRationales.c: This option makes Use the line for routine blood draws to avoid extra venipunctures the focus, when the bedside priority is driven by Consistent hub disinfection and sterile dressing care are core nursing actions for preventing central line infections. Treats an active warning sign as something safe to watch, so That choice could make the nurse miss the window for infection or sepsis escalation while infection progression progresses.
distractorRationales.d: Leave one port uncapped between intermittent infusions fits a lower-risk interpretation, instead of responding to Dressing changes should follow evidence-based timing and contamination rules, not patient comfort alone. Does not account for the clinical cue most likely to deteriorate first, so That mismatch risks slower recognition of infection progression and postpones the needed nursing response.

questions.takeaway: Preventing CLABSI depends on aseptic access and sterile site care every time.
